summary: Paul Graham traces how the Swiss watch industry's collapse in the 1970s forced mechanical watchmakers to abandon engineering excellence and reinvent themselves as luxury brands, using the case study to illuminate a broader force: when technology commoditizes substantive differences between products, brand fills the vacuum. He argues this transformation — from form-follows-function to form-follows-brand — produces increasingly strange, dysfunctional outcomes, and draws a philosophical lesson about how to find meaningful work in any era.
key_points: Brand is what's left when substantive differences between products disappear, and technology naturally tends to make those differences disappear
Branding and good design are fundamentally opposed: good design converges on right answers, while branding must diverge to be distinctive
Quality doesn't stop mattering in a brand age — it becomes a threshold that must be met to maintain credibility, but no longer sells the product itself
Fields have golden ages defined by smart people working on interesting problems; participants rarely recognize them until they're over
The way to find golden ages is not to seek them directly, but to follow interesting problems
money_quotes: Brand is what's left when the substantive differences between products disappear. But making the substantive differences between products disappear is what technology naturally tends to do.
Branding is centrifugal; design is centripetal.
When you have a world defined only by brand, it's going to be a weird, bad world.
Follow the problems. The way to find golden ages is not to go looking for them.
What a golden age feels like, at the time, is just that smart people are working hard on interesting problems and getting results.
tone: analytical and cautionary, with moments of dark wit
key_insight: When technology commoditizes a field's core function, brand fills the void — but brand's demands are fundamentally opposed to good design, producing increasingly strange and hollow outcomes; the antidote is to follow interesting problems rather than lucrative brands.
